ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indonesian crispy fried duck (Bebek Goreng). FOODON_03302051 (fried duck food product) is used as the closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Duck meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for deep-fried spiced duck in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Bebek Goreng or fried duck as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 quarter duck with kremes and Indomie
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of a quarter duck (approximately 250 g cooked duck meat with skin and bone) accompanied by kremes (crispy flour fritters, ~50 g) and a portion of Indomie instant noodles (~50 g cooked), totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA data for deep-fried duck and Indomie noodle component
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for duck meat and skin, cooked (NDB No. 05140), adjusted upward for deep-frying fat absorption (~10–15% additional fat per 100 g cooked weight). Kremes component (deep-fried flour fritters) and Indomie noodle component contribute additional carbohydrate and sodium. Composite per-100g values represent the mixed dish as served. Sodium elevated to reflect Indomie seasoning packet contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bebek Goreng. GI estimated at 40 based on analogous mixed meat-and-fried-starch dishes with moderate carbohydrate content. The carbohydrate component (kremes flour fritters and Indomie noodles) is the primary glycemic driver. Fried wheat-flour products typically have GI in the range 35–50 (low to medium). A conservative GI of 40 (low category) is applied. GL per serving calculated as: (40 × 29.75 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 11.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Indomie seasoning packet and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Bebek Goreng as served with Indomie, since the sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g, ~1470 mg per full serving) can represent over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting the dish without the Indomie seasoning packet significantly reduces sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from kremes and Indomie noodles with low-to-moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Bebek Goreng in controlled portions, as the GI is estimated at 40 (low category); however, the carbohydrate contribution from kremes and Indomie noodles (~29.75 g per serving) should be counted toward the meal carbohydrate budget, and the high fat content may impair postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying duck with skin and Indomie seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Bebek Goreng infrequently due to its high saturated fat (~5.8 g per 100 g), cholesterol (~82 mg per 100 g), and sodium content; removing the skin before eating and omitting the Indomie seasoning packet can meaningfully reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bebek Goreng (Crispy Fried Duck) is a classic Indonesian dish consisting of a quarter duck that is marinated in a fragrant spice paste — typically including turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, garlic, shallots, and coriander — before being deep-fried until the skin is golden and exceptionally crispy. The dish is a staple of Indonesian cuisine, particularly associated with the Javanese and Madurese culinary traditions, and is widely served across Indonesia and in Indonesian restaurants throughout Southeast Asia, including Singapore. In this field survey data variant from Smile Martabak, the duck is served with kremes (light, crispy deep-fried flour fritters that add textural contrast) and Indomie instant noodles, making it a hearty and filling meal. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, aromatic, and rich, with the crispy skin providing a satisfying crunch against the tender, well-seasoned duck meat. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~19.5 g per 100 g) and fat (~20.5 g per 100 g), with moderate carbohydrate from the kremes and noodle components. The sodium content is notably elevated due to the Indomie seasoning packet, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low category), driven primarily by the fried starch components. Bebek Goreng is generally considered halal and dairy-free but contains gluten from the Indomie noodles and kremes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy fried duck?
What is the glycemic index of fried duck?
What spices are used in Bebek Goreng?
What is kremes served with duck?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Bebek Goreng as a composite dish (with kremes and Indomie) was found in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy duck meat and skin data, adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption and the kremes and Indomie components. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates for the full dish as served (duck quarter with kremes and Indomie), expressed per 100 g of the mixed dish. The USDA SR Legacy duck (cooked, meat and skin) base values were adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption and the carbohydrate/sodium contribution of kremes and Indomie instant noodles. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy duck entry and is not reliably estimated for this composite dish. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting small amounts naturally present in duck fat; this is not an industrial trans fat. 4. Sodium is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) primarily due to the Indomie seasoning packet; the dish without Indomie would have substantially lower sodium. 5. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on fried wheat-starch products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this dish. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the Indonesian Muslim culinary tradition and the restaurant context; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy duck and standard fried-starch component data and should be treated as approximate. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite dish.

Applied analogous GI estimate of 40 from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for fried wheat-starch mixed dish.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg as -1 with explicit note referencing source non-reporting.
Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
